text.title: Filesystem Backup with Restic Failure Modes
text.summary: 3 antipatterns observed in production. Each pairs symptom + root-cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.symptom: RESTIC_PASSWORD literal in script or .env tracked by Git; on rotation, all snapshots become undecryptable.
antipattern.root-cause: Author treated repo password as 'shared secret' and inlined it; no secrets-manager integration.
antipattern.fix: Move to Vault / AWS Secrets Manager; rotate the leaked password; rewrite repo if exposure is confirmed; add pre-commit `git diff --staged | grep RESTIC_PASSWORD`.

antipattern.symptom: `restic forget --keep-daily 7` runs nightly, snapshot count stays at 7, but S3 storage grows linearly with no plateau.
antipattern.root-cause: Operator wrote forget without `--prune`, expecting it to GC; forget only removes refs.
antipattern.fix: Replace with `restic forget --keep-daily 7 --keep-weekly 4 --keep-monthly 12 --prune` and run a one-off `restic prune` to reclaim historical waste.

antipattern.symptom: Autonomous backup agent emits `restic forget --prune` and accidentally drops a snapshot still needed for in-flight restore.
antipattern.root-cause: Author wired the agent to a destructive operation with no approval gate.
antipattern.fix: Restrict the agent to `restic forget --dry-run`; require a human-approval ticket before the real prune; log the intended retention diff.
